Now again, you've heard me say this before, my why, when I made my mistakes and went to jail, people always say, how are you able to accomplish what you accomplished when you were behind bars and just most people, they falter and fade away or at least just spend whatever time they're in jail doing nothing, just sort of not bettering themselves. Yet I came out infinitely strong. I wrote the book, at least the precursor to the book, The Wolf of Wall Street, which served as the springboard for everything that came after. Yes, I invented the straight line a little before that, but it was that work I did, perfecting my writing skills, not taking no for an answer, not using the mistakes I made as a reason, focusing more on the result, doing the work, getting strong in my winter. I emerged an infinitely more powerful person because my ability to harness the power of the written word, not just a spoken word. You get it? But what allowed me to do that was my why. In other words, when I was alone at night in my bed, my uncomfortable bed thinking I couldn't go on, I will tell you, I felt like that many, many nights, alone with my thoughts, nothing to do but just to really dwell on my mistakes, I'd close my eyes and I'd imagine the faces of my two children. And that was my why, to come back to them, to make things right for them. Well, again, I said it before, we'll always do more for those we love unconditionally, for causes we believe in. Because what I don't want from you right now is to write that some bullshit vision that's impossible for you to achieve. Because then your brain says bullshit, bullshit has no power. So there's a balancing act, there's some art to this of not dreaming to the point where you're, I want to, I dream because I want to launch a rocket to Mars and be the first person to cultivate an asteroid for gold. I say, I want to, I dream really big and I said, it's great expectation. So if I'm only half right, I'm doing great. The last thing you want to do is like, you know, you said a target, if you're, if you're 50%, you're like, oh, it's not great. Be half right. And you'd be like, if I'm half right, it would be great, but it's got to be reality. James Cameron, when they interviewed him, he was being interviewed about, you know, how does this one director have the two highest grossing movies in history? Titanic and Avatar. One guy took him 15 years to make Avatar, 15 years, until he got it all lined up. And when they asked him what his secret to success was, he says, because I dream so big, I said, I wanted this movie to do, to do a billion dollars. I dream so big that even if I'm half right, it's still amazing. It's a great interview. That's a philosophy that creates massive wealth and success and empowerment in your life because you're chasing your own great vision. But of course, James Cameron possesses the skill sets. It's not delusion. He's done the work. He knows how to direct. He knows lighting. He knows how to write. He owns the skills. You got to be willing to do the work and learn what you got to learn.
